title: Patrick McGovern, Stephen Hill, Colin Mills, and Michael White: Market, Class, and Employment
abstract: Conventional wisdom and much social science research holds that work and employment relations have been dramatically transformed in the past three decades in liberal market economies such as Britain and the United States. A popular view—advanced prominently by Peter Cappelli and colleagues in the United States (1997), and by Damien Grimshaw and Jill Rubery in Britain (1998)—describes a ‘new economic order’ that is characterized by a growing marketization of the employment relationship, halting if not reversing the long-term trend towards internalization. Others have argued that class has declined as a source of inequality in these nations: the (late) sociologist Aage Sørensen (2000) claimed that individual characteristics such as education have become more important for inequality in the United States due to the increased marketization of the employment relationship; and the British economic historian Alice Russell (1998) argued that class is becoming less significant in Britain due to a long-term internalization of the employment relationship.
